title: Modi's First Ethiopia Visit: India Deepens Global South Partnership Strategy
lede: Prime Minister Narendra Modi arrived in Addis Ababa on December 16, 2025, for his first bilateral visit to Ethiopia, signaling India's commitment to strengthening partnerships within the Global South.

sections.context: Prime Minister Modi's visit to the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia, undertaken at the invitation of Ethiopian Prime Minister Dr. Abiy Ahmed Ali, marks a significant diplomatic milestone. According to India's Ministry of External Affairs, Ethiopia holds particular strategic importance as a partner in the Global South. The timing of this first bilateral visit underscores India's deliberate prioritization of engagement with influential African nations. The visit's focus on bilateral partnership strengthening suggests India views Ethiopia not merely as a bilateral partner but as a platform for advancing collective Global South interests. This positioning reflects a broader Indian diplomatic strategy that extends beyond traditional bilateral frameworks.
sections.threads: India's Ethiopia engagement fits within a larger pattern of high-level visits cementing Global South partnerships. Similar strategic recalibrations are evident across India's diplomatic calendar, particularly in how New Delhi courts influence among developing nations. The MEA's emphasis on strengthening partnership 'not just for ourselves, but also for the larger community in the Global South' suggests a multilateral dimension to bilateral visits. Ethiopia's significance as host to the African Union and other continental institutions adds institutional weight to this engagement. The presence of Secretary (ER) Sudhakar Dalela, Ambassador Anil Rai, and Joint Secretary (E&SA) Janesh Kain at the briefing indicates the visit's treatment as a whole-of-government diplomatic initiative rather than a ceremonial engagement.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the outcomes of this first bilateral visit closely, particularly what substantive agreements emerge from Modi-Abiy discussions. The MEA's framing suggests deliverables are expected—institutional frameworks, trade partnerships, or multilateral coordination mechanisms. Watch for follow-up statements on Africa-India partnership architecture and whether Ethiopia becomes a hub for India's continental African strategy. The visit's success in producing concrete deliverables will signal India's capacity to translate strategic positioning into tangible partnership outcomes. Future visits by Ethiopian leadership to India, or India's participation in African Union initiatives, will indicate whether this visit catalyzes sustained institutional engagement or remains symbolic.
